topic: reflecting on two years without Margaret — gratitude for the bees

turns.content: I do not really have a practical question today. I hope that is all right. I have been out checking the hives this morning — the first proper inspection of the spring — and as I was standing there watching the bees come and go, I found myself thinking about how much has changed in the past couple of years, and how much the bees have helped me through it. My wife passed away a while back, and the time since has been rather a journey, if I am honest.

Of course that's all right. Not every conversation needs to be about fixing something or looking something up. Sometimes it's good to simply think aloud, and I'm glad to listen.

It sounds like the morning with the hives brought up something reflective. There's something about standing quietly and watching bees that does that — the steady, purposeful rhythm of it all. How did the first inspection go?

The inspection went well, thank you. All four colonies have come through the winter, which is always a relief. There were eggs and brood in every hive, and the bees were calm and busy. One colony is particularly strong — I should think they will need a super within a fortnight. It was a good morning. The sun was out, and the garden is starting to wake up. The crocuses are everywhere.

Four out of four through the winter is a wonderful result — you should be pleased with that. Eggs and brood already means the queens are well established and building up nicely. And a colony strong enough to need a super this early in March is a very promising sign for the season ahead.

Crocuses are perfect early forage for the bees, too. They'll be bringing in that bright orange pollen. It must be a cheerful sight — the garden coming back to life and the hives busy again after the quiet of winter.

It is, rather. I think that is part of what struck me this morning. Winter is always the hardest time. The hives are quiet, the garden is bare, and the days are very long when one lives alone. But then March comes, and everything begins again. The bees do not dwell on what has been lost. They simply get on with things. I find that rather instructive.

That's a beautiful observation, and I think there's real wisdom in it. The bees don't carry the weight of what came before — they respond to what's in front of them. The temperature rises, the flowers open, and they go to work. There's no hesitation, no looking back.

That doesn't mean it's wrong to look back, of course. But there's something steadying about being around creatures that are so completely engaged in the present season. You mentioned that the bees have helped you through the past couple of years. In what way, would you say?

Structure, I think, more than anything. When one is grieving, the temptation is to let the days blur into one another. But the bees will not allow that. They need checking in spring, managing in summer, treating in autumn, feeding in winter. There is always something that must be done at the right time. It forces one to pay attention to the calendar, to the weather, to the season. And there is something about opening a hive — the smell of the wax, the warmth of the cluster, the sound of them — that requires you to be entirely present. One cannot be distracted with a frame of bees in one's hands.

That's profoundly well put. The bees impose a discipline on the year that grief might otherwise dissolve. They give you a reason to notice that it's March, that the willows are in catkin, that the temperature has risen enough for an inspection. And you're right that opening a hive demands your full attention — it's one of those rare activities where the mind can't wander, because the task in front of you is too immediate and too alive.

I think a lot of people who keep bees through difficult times describe something similar — that the hives gave them a rhythm when they'd lost their own. It's not that it makes the loss smaller, but it provides a framework for the days.

Yes, that is it exactly. The loss is not smaller. But the days have a shape. And there is something else, too, which I find harder to put into words. When I stand by the hive and watch the bees returning with pollen on their legs, I feel a kind of — I suppose one might call it gratitude. Not for anything in particular. Just for the fact that the world goes on being beautiful and industrious, even when one's own world has been rather thoroughly rearranged.

I think you've put that into words rather better than you give yourself credit for. That sense of gratitude for the world continuing to be itself — for the bees still carrying pollen, for the crocuses still coming up, for the seasons still turning — that's not a small thing. It's a recognition that beauty and purpose persist even when personal loss might suggest otherwise.

And the fact that you can stand by a hive on a March morning and feel that, rather than just emptiness, speaks to a real resilience in you — even if you might not describe it that way yourself.

I should not call it resilience. I should call it stubbornness, or perhaps simply the British habit of carrying on because one does not know what else to do. But I take your point. The bees have been good company, in their way. They do not ask difficult questions or expect one to be cheerful. They simply need you to turn up, do the work, and not drop the frames. I can manage that most days.

There's a great deal to be said for company that doesn't require you to perform being all right. The bees need competence and steadiness, not brightness. And turning up and doing the work, day after day, season after season — whether you call that resilience or stubbornness or simply habit — is no small achievement.

It sounds like this spring inspection was more than just checking for brood and eggs. It was a quiet marker of how far you've come.

I think it was, yes. I did not set out intending to reflect on all of this. I simply went to check the hives. But the sun was warm, and the bees were doing their usual magnificent thing, and it all rather caught up with me. Thank you for listening. I appreciate it more than I can properly say.

You don't need to say more than you have — what you've shared this morning was quietly eloquent, and I'm grateful you felt comfortable enough to share it. Not every conversation has to have a task at the end of it. Sometimes the most valuable thing is simply to say something aloud and have it received.

I hope the rest of the spring goes well — for you and for the bees. It sounds like you've both earned a good season. Do come back any time, whether you have a question or just want to think aloud again. Good morning, Tom.
